old_claim.note: Industrial Parts Catalogs provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains because early industrial designers produced catalogs and corporate graphics as design outputs, rather than depending on industrial parts catalogs as a prerequisite.

source_shape.source_locator: Britannica says Behrens was appointed artistic adviser for all AEG products in 1907 and that his contributions included AEG catalogs, office stationery, products, shops, and factories.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source treats catalogs as one of Behrens's design outputs for AEG, alongside products and corporate materials.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: That reverses the old edge direction: catalogs belong inside the industrial-design output set, while the prerequisite should be the mass-produced product context.

ontology_before: Industrial Design Profession depended on Industrial Parts Catalogs, implying parts-catalog practice enabled the profession.
ontology_after: Industrial Design Profession is anchored to mass-produced product design; catalogs are treated as possible designed artifacts, not a prerequisite technology.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: industrial_design_profession no longer directly depends on industrial_parts_catalogs.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped specifically to catalog-design offices rather than industrial design practice.
A source showed industrial parts catalogs directly preceded and enabled Behrens-style product design as a professional field.
The industrial_parts_catalogs node were broadened to represent all corporate product-design communication.
short_reason: Catalogs were an industrial-design output here, not the prerequisite.
